items.title: Middle East crisis live: Iran warns it will ‘open new fronts’ against US if attacks resume after Trump suspends strikes
items.standfirst: The US president said he called off a planned attack on Iran on Tuesday so that peace talks could continueFull report: Trump claims planned attack on Iran postponed after Tehran makes new proposal to end warIran’s army has warned it would “open new fronts” against the US if it resumes attacks on the country amid reports that Donald Trump is weighing up restarting military operations in Iran amid an impasse in negotiations.“If the enemy is foolish enough to fall into the Zionist trap again and launches new aggression against our beloved Iran, we will open new fronts against it, with new equipment and new methods,” army spokesperson Mohammad Akraminia said, according to Iran’s ISNA news agency. Continue reading...
items.content: The US president said he called off a planned attack on Iran on Tuesday so that peace talks could continueFull report: Trump claims planned attack on Iran postponed after Tehran makes new proposal to end warIran’s army has warned it would “open new fronts” against the US if it resumes attacks on the country amid reports that Donald Trump is weighing up restarting military operations in Iran amid an impasse in negotiations.“If the enemy is foolish enough to fall into the Zionist trap again and launches new aggression against our beloved Iran, we will open new fronts against it, with new equipment and new methods,” army spokesperson Mohammad Akraminia said, according to Iran’s ISNA news agency. items.content: President Trump says he scrapped a planned attack on Iran at the request of Gulf allies, as "serious negotiations" on a peace deal are underway.

items.title: Trump says holding off on strike on Iran at request of Gulf allies
items.standfirst: After issuing thinly veiled threats against Iran over the weekend, US President Trump appeared to strike a different tone on May 18. He described ongoing peace talks with Tehran as “very positive” and even claimed he had paused a planned US military strike.
items.content: After issuing thinly veiled threats against Iran over the weekend, US President Trump appeared to strike a different tone on May 18. He described ongoing peace talks with Tehran as “very positive” and even claimed he had paused a planned US military strike.

items.content: The US president says he is holding off on an attack planned for Tuesday as "serious negotiations are now taking place".

items.title: Trump claims planned attack on Iran postponed after Tehran makes new proposal to end war
items.standfirst: The US president says plans for a US military strike on Iran have been paused because ‘serious negotiations are now taking place’Iran has made a new proposal for a deal to definitively end the war in the Middle East, officials in the region said on Monday, with Donald Trump claiming he had postponed new military strikes so talks could continue.But while the US president has regularly used social media to threaten Tehran, and to claim that a peace deal was within reach, there has been no sign of an immediate breakthrough in the stalled negotiations to end the war. Continue reading...
items.content: The US president says plans for a US military strike on Iran have been paused because ‘serious negotiations are now taking place’Iran has made a new proposal for a deal to definitively end the war in the Middle East, officials in the region said on Monday, with Donald Trump claiming he had postponed new military strikes so talks could continue.But while the US president has regularly used social media to threaten Tehran, and to claim that a peace deal was within reach, there has been no sign of an immediate breakthrough in the stalled negotiations to end the war. Continue reading...

items.title: Trump says he called off a planned attack on Iran
items.standfirst: President Donald Trump said he is calling off an attack on Iran planned for Tuesday because regional leaders had urged him to allow negotiations to continue and a “very acceptable” deal for the U.S. was at hand.
items.content: President Donald Trump said he is calling off an attack on Iran planned for Tuesday because regional leaders had urged him to allow negotiations to continue and a “very acceptable” deal for the U.S. was at hand.

The shooting is being investigated as a hate crime, San Diego Police Chief Scott Wahl said

items.title: Shooting at Islamic Center of San Diego being investigated as hate crime
items.standfirst: The Islamic Center of San Diego shooting, where three were killed on Monday, is being investigated as a hate crime as more details emerge about the suspected shooters. Jonathan Vigliotti reports, and Michael Alcazar, a professor at John Jay College of Criminal Justice, joins CBS News with more insight.
items.content: The Islamic Center of San Diego shooting, where three were killed on Monday, is being investigated as a hate crime as more details emerge about the suspected shooters. Jonathan Vigliotti reports, and Michael Alcazar, a professor at John Jay College of Criminal Justice, joins CBS News with more insight.

items.title: Teen suspect's mom alerted police before deadly mosque shooting

CBS News' Anna Schecter reports.

items.title: Three dead in San Diego shooting as police investigate possible hate crime
items.standfirst: Police were investigating a shooting in the US state of California as a hate crime on May 19 after a pair of teenage gunmen killed three people at a mosque complex. A security guard at the Islamic center is among the three victims, with authorities saying that his response had helped prevent a deadlier attack. FrANCE 24's Wassim Cornet tells us more.
items.content: Police were investigating a shooting in the US state of California as a hate crime on May 19 after a pair of teenage gunmen killed three people at a mosque complex. A security guard at the Islamic center is among the three victims, with authorities saying that his response had helped prevent a deadlier attack. FrANCE 24's Wassim Cornet tells us more.

items.title: Deadly attack by teen gunmen on a San Diego mosque
items.standfirst: Police raced Monday to catch an armed teenage runaway before he and another teen opened fire on a San Diego mosque, killing three men and then themselves. The shooting is being investigated as a hate crime, San Diego Police Chief Scott Wahl said.
items.content: Police raced Monday to catch an armed teenage runaway before he and another teen opened fire on a San Diego mosque, killing three men and then themselves. The shooting is being investigated as a hate crime, San Diego Police Chief Scott Wahl said.

items.title: San Diego police treating mosque shooting as hate crime
items.standfirst: Two teenage shooters opened fire at a mosque in San Diego, killing three, before killing themselves a few blocks away. The police is now investigating a hate crime: no specific threats have been made against the Islamic centre of San Diego but the authorities found evidence that the suspects engaged in generalised hate rhetoric.
items.content: Two teenage shooters opened fire at a mosque in San Diego, killing three, before killing themselves a few blocks away. The police is now investigating a hate crime: no specific threats have been made against the Islamic centre of San Diego but the authorities found evidence that the suspects engaged in generalised hate rhetoric.

items.title: China projects itself as a "stable power" as it greets US and Russia in less than a week
items.standfirst: Russia's President Vladimir Putin is visiting his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping, less than a week after US President Donald Trump's high-profile visit. The talks are likely to be dominated by the trade relations between the two countries, with China presenting a lifeline for sanctioned Russia. FRANCE 24's Jan Camenzind Broomby tells us more from Taiwan.
items.content: Russia's President Vladimir Putin is visiting his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping, less than a week after US President Donald Trump's high-profile visit. Putin will travel to China on May 19 and 20, less than a week after US President Donald Trump met Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing. August Hakansson looks at whats at stake.

items.title: Son of billionaire Mango founder detained over father’s fatal mountain fall
items.standfirst: Fashion chain boss Isak Andic died while on a hiking trip with his son Jonathan two years ago
items.content: Fashion chain boss Isak Andic died while on a hiking trip with his son Jonathan two years ago

items.title: Son of Mango fashion chain founder arrested in Spain over death of father
items.standfirst: Catalan police questioning Jonathan Andic over father Isak Andic’s apparent fall down a mountain ravine in 2024Police in Catalonia have arrested the son of Isak Andic, the founder of the fashion chain Mango, and are questioning him in connection with the death of his father in the mountains near Barcelona almost 18 months ago.Andic, who was 71, died in December 2024 after apparently falling 100 metres down a ravine while hiking in Montserrat with his son, Jonathan. His death prompted tributes from politicians, journalists and the fashion world. Continue reading...

items.title: Dutch authorities expect more cruise ship outbreaks as hantavirus-hit vessel docks

items.title: High Speed 2 to Cost up to £103 Billion After More Delays
items.standfirst: The UK’s High Speed 2 rail line will cost between £87.7 billion ($117 billion) and £102.7 billion and open by 2036 at the earliest, Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander said, revealing the latest budget increase and delay to the troubled project.
items.content: The UK’s High Speed 2 rail line will cost between £87.7 billion ($117 billion) and £102.7 billion and open by 2036 at the earliest, Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander said, revealing the latest budget increase and delay to the troubled project.

items.title: HS2 bill could rise to £102bn with first trains delayed until 2039, government admits
items.standfirst: Transport secretary Heidi Alexander blames Conservative government for ‘obscene increase in times and costs’Business live – latest updatesThe HS2 high-speed railway will now cost up to £102.7bn and trains will not start running between London and Birmingham until as late as 2039, the government has admitted – £70bn more and 13 years later than originally promised.The transport secretary, Heidi Alexander, said that the truncated railway would not be entirely completed until as late as 2043. Continue reading...
